en.name: SMart Industrial Tourism in the Mediterranean
en.description:
Before COVID-19, tourism accounted for about 15% of Med countries GDP and 11.5% of their total employment. With the lockdown and travel restrictions the pandemic totally disrupted the sector. Now that the pandemic is subsiding and the sector is recovering, a new risk has arisen, that old unsustainable tourism patterns are re-proposed, generating undesired effects like over-tourism and seasonalization, with their unbearable pressure on the environment and the communities. 

The challenge in the post-pandemic time is to change tourism, by e.g. focusing on alternative destinations and by exploiting technology (particularly those that have been boosted by the pandemic) to make the tourist experience more rewarding and yet more sustainable.

Many Med regions and cities with important industrial sites are considering Industrial Tourism as a way to attract tourists to their areas. On the other hand, the common-place visit to productive sites could be greatly enhanced by integrating additional cultural and historical elements through the use of advanced and immersive technologies that can make the experience more informative and enticing for the visitor (Smart Industrial Tourism).

The project aims to exploit transnational cooperation to explore the social and economic potential of Smart Industrial Tourism (SMIT), as a sustainable economic activity that can benefit tourism actors, SMEs, the industry, CCIs, R&D centers, HEIs and technology providers, and the local communities.

The project will create transnational working groups involving 4helix stakeholders to explore industrial tourism and related advanced technologies applications. It will eventually define a common strategy and Action Plan for the development of SMIT in the Med regions. The result will be a diversification of the economy and of the sector, a reduction of over-tourism and seasonalization, and a boost to social and economic growth and to technological innovation of tourism at large.
en.expected_achievements:
Assess the social & economic potential of Smart Industrial Tourism (SMIT) in the partner regions by analzying their tourism systems, GPs and advanced technologies' applications. Deliver a joint SWOT analysis of sustainable SMIT in the Med area.
Co-design a transnational strategy and an Action Plan to develop SMIT in the Med area, by using the knowledge-base of WP1 to identify key development areas through an Entrepreneurial Discovery Process based on a wide 4helix stakeholders involvement.
To set the basis for the common demonstration methodology and contribute to the transferring of project results by contributing to the Result amplification strategy of the Interreg Euro-MED Programme.
en.actual_achievements:
Following the project approval, the Lead partner Municipality of Prato (MoP) established the project management team and set up administrative and financial procedures within the internal organisational structure. At the very beginning, the LP coordinated the implementation of the pre-contracting procedure by gathering information from project partners and providing inputs to the Interreg Euro-MED secretariat. The subsidy contract was signed by the legal representative. The  partnership agreement  was signed by all partners. 

After the pre-contracting phase was completed, the kick off workshop and the 1st steering committee (SC) meeting were organised. The two day event took place in Prato on 5-6 March, 2024. During the event,  the project was presented to the local and regional stakeholders, as well as the general public.  During the SC meeting, Steering Committee regulation was discussed and approved by the partnership.  

The first phase of the project was focused on assessing the social & economic potential of Smart Industrial Tourism in the partner regions by analysing their tourism systems and collecting of existing good practices  in industrial tourism. 
Based on a template for the country survey provided by PP2, ISCTE-Lisboa partners collected data on industrial tourism that include information on the current state of Industrial Tourism, government policies and support, data and statistics, technology integration, marketing & promotion and the future outlook.  The first draft of the Country surveys that are the base for the D.1.1.1 Baseline study of tourism systems in the partner regions,  were completed in the first semester. In parallel, partners started the collection of good practices that will feed  the  D.1.2.1 Catalogue of Industrial Tourism Practices. According to what was agreed during the 1st SC meeting, the sample of GPs is as follows: Italy: 20 responses, Spain: 25 responses, Slovenia: 15 responses, Greece: 15 responses, Croatia: 15 responses, Portugal: 15 responses. At the time of drafting the 1st report, all partners collected the foreseen number of GPs and both deliverables are being finalised. 

In June PP2 ISCTE hosted the 2nd Steering Committee and technical meeting in Lisbon (18 – 19 June 2024). During the meeting partners discussed the 1st results of the WP1 and the most appropriate methodology for the implementation of WP2 activities, in particular the stakeholders engagement that will take place in Semester 2 and the organisation of the upcoming Innovation Camps.  A public workshop, entitled “Challenges and opportunities of Industrial Tourism in Portugal” was organised on 19th June, with the participation of relevant stakeholders and the scientific community.  

Throughout the semester, partners participated also in the activities organised by the Thematic Project of the ST Community of the Interreg Euro-MED. The LP representatives participated in several online meetings, delivered initial project information and documentation and invited the Barcelona Provincial Council to the project kick off meeting in Prato. MoP also participated in the Kick-off meeting of the Community that took place in Rome on the 10 and 11 of April. 

PP4 MDT, coordinator communication activities set up the social media (Facebook, LinkedIn) and the project website. 19 Facebook posts, 18 LinkedIn posts and 5 news articles on the web site were published during the first semester. 1 press release, resulting in 7 articles were published on occasion of the kick off meeting. All partners contributed in drafting of the contents and disseminated information about the project through their communication channels. Partners published a dedicated web page on their institutional web sites and placed the project poster in their premises. 

PP6 E- Zavod assigned a carbon footprint manager that presented the Carbon Footprint  activity and templates to partners and instructed them on how to collect the data. Partners collected the data for the 1st semester and submitted it to PP6 for further analysis and reporting . The first results will be reported in the full report (after M12). 

In the first year of activities, LP Municipality of Prato (MoP) has established the project management team and set up the administrative and financial procedures for the internal organisation, through which the approved workplan has been coordinated and implemented. 

The kick off and the 1st steering committee (SC) meeting were organised in Prato on the 5 - 6 of March 2024. On this occasion, the project was presented to the stakeholders and the general public.  At the 1st SC, its regulation was approved. 

The 1st year mostly focused on WP1, that is, on the assessment of the social & economic potential of Smart Industrial Tourism in the partner regions (see: D.1.1.1 Baseline study of tourism systems  in the partner regions). The partners' tourism systems were analysed and existing GPs collected in 2 dedicated catalogues (see: D1.2.1 Catalogue of Industrial Tourism Practices and D1.3.1 Catalogue of Advanced Technologies Apllications for Tourism). 

By using the templates developed by PP2 ISCTE for the country surveys, the partners reported the current state of Industrial Tourism in their respective regions, their governmental policies and support, data and statistics, technology integration, marketing & promotion, and future outlooks.  The overall survey was finalised as "D.1.1.1 Baseline study of tourism systems in the partner regions".

Through another template, GPs were collected by the partners for "D1.2.1 Catalogue of Industrial Tourism Practices", which includes several GPs focusing on successful experiences in Industrial Tourism. 

At the 2nd Steering Committee and technical meeting hosted by PP2 ISCTE in Lisbon (18 – 19 June 2024), the partners reviewed the outcomes of WP1 as a basis to kick-start WP2 activities, particularly as regards the engagement of stakeholders and their participation in the Innovation Camps.  The public workshop on “Challenges and opportunities of Industrial Tourism in Portugal” organised on the 19th June in Lisbon, saw the participation of relevant tourism stakeholders and the scientific community.

Regarding "A1.3 Application of advanced technologies in the tourism sector", partner in charge PP7 Faculty of Economics and Business Rijeka Hrvatska (HR) EFRI, provided a dedicated template after the summer break, whereby partners collected GPs in the area of Advanced Technologies Applications for Tourism. According to EFRI's instructions, at least 5 GPs were collected by each partner in the following areas: Virtual Reality (VR), Augmented Reality (AR), Artificial Intelligence (AI), Chatbots, Internet of Things (IoT). The information has been included in "SMITour WP1 D1.3.1 Catalogue of Advanced Technologies Apllications for Tourism ".

In the 2nd semester "WP2 Smart Industrial Tourism development strategy and Action Plan for the

2nd Innovation Camp I 5 - 6 March 2025, Athens

On the occasion of the SC meeting in Maribor, MoP delivered a first proposal for the structure and the contents of the WP2 outputs, O2.1 Common Strategy for the development of Smart Industrial Tourism in the Mediterranean and O2.2 Action Plan for developing Smart Industrial Tourism in the Mediterranean. 

Throughout the semester, partners participated in the activities organised by the Thematic Project of the ST Community of the Interreg Euro-MED. The LP representatives participated in several online meetings. The Municipality of Prato also participated at the event organised by several Interreg programmes on sustainable tourism in Split (25-26 June), entitled “Paths that last”

PP4 MDT, communication coordinator for the project, regularly updating the website with both news and upcoming events related to SMITour activities, including meetings, focus groups, and innovation camps.  SMITour social media channels (Facebook and LinkedIn) were regularly updated on a weekly basis. This included creating content specifically about the project and its activities (such as meeting follow-ups), while also supporting content, events, and meetings from the Interreg Euro-MED Program and the Sustainable Tourism Mission. fr.name: SMart Industrial Tourism in the Mediterranean
fr.description:
Avant le COVID-19, le tourisme représentait environ 15 % du PIB des Pays Med et l’11,5 % de leur emploi total. Avec les restrictions de voyage la pandémie a totalement perturbé le secteur. Maintenant que la pandémie s'est calmée et que le secteur s’est rétabli, un nouveau risque apparu est que les vieux modèles de tourisme non durables soient reproposés, en générant des effets indésirables - surtourisme et saisonnalisation avec leur pression sur l'environnement et les communautés.
En cette période post-pandémique le défi est de changer le tourisme, en se concentrant sur des destinations alternatives et en exploitant les technologies (en particulier celles qui ont été stimulées par la pandémie) pour rendre l'expérience touristique plus gratifiante et durable.
De nombreuses régions et villes Med ayant des sites industriels importants, considérent le tourisme industriel comme un moyen d'attirer les touristes. D'autre part, la visite conventionelle à des sites productifs pourrait être grandement améliorée en intégrant des éléments culturels et historiques supplémentaires grâce à l'utilisation de technologies avancées et immersives (Tourisme Industrial Intelligent - TII).
Le projet vise à exploiter la coopération transnationale pour explorer le potentiel social et économique du TII, en tant que l’activité économique durable peut bénéficier aux acteurs du tourisme, aux PME, à l'industrie, aux CCI, aux fournisseurs de technologies et aux communautés locales.
Le projet créera des groupes de travail transnationaux pour explorer les bonnes pratiques dans le tourisme industriel et dans l'utilisation de technologies avancées et immersives. Il définira une stratégie et un plan d'action communs pour le développement du TII dans les régions Med. Le résultat sera une diversification du secteur, une réduction du surtourisme et de la saisonnalisation, ainsi qu'un coup de pouce à la croissance sociale et économique et à l'innovation technologique du tourisme dans son ensemble.
fr.expected_achievements:
Analyze social and economic potential of smart industrial tourism (SMIT) in the partner regions by profiling their tourism systems, GPs and advanced technologies' applications. Deliver a SWOT analysis for developing sustainable SMIT in the Med area.
Co-design a trans-regional strategy to develop SMIT in the Med area. The strategy will use the knowledge-base developed in WP1 to identify key development areas through an Entrepreneurial Discovery Process (EDP) based on wide stakeholder involvement.
To set the basis for the common demonstration methodology and contribute to the transferring of project results by contributing to the Result amplification strategy of the Interreg Euro-MED Programme.

fr.name: Planification des services de transport autonomes à la demande dans l'espace Interreg Euro-MED
fr.description:
Dans la zone Euro-MED, 40.6M d’habitants vivent dans des territoires de faible densité avec peu ou pas de transports publics (IEMED, 2021) en raison du coût de ces services rapporté au nombre d’usagers par kilomètre parcouru. Cette absence de transports publics conduit à l’isolement des populations ou à une forte dépendance automobile (ESPON, 2020) entrainant d’importantes émissions de CO2 (156M de tonnes) et des problématiques d’accès à l’emploi et aux services publics.

Les recherches et initiatives européennes ont mis en avant le potentiel des Transports autonomes à la Demande (TaD) pour offrir un service de transport public de qualité avec des couts opérationnels réduits (41% d’amélioration du rapport qualité/prix, Malmö Uni. 2022) et un moindre impact environnemental (baisse des émissions de 72% selon K2Centrum, 2022). Malheureusement, il est difficile pour les collectivités et les entreprises de transport public de mettre en place un service autonome à la demande sans connaitre au préalable l’impact que celui-ci aura à l'échelle locale.

Les projets AVENUE (H2020) et MultiDEPART (EIT UM) ont développé des méthodologies pour calculer l’impact local des TaD mais ces dernières restent difficilement exploitables directement par les décideurs publics. ArtMED a pour objectif l’optimisation d’un outil d’évaluation d’impact de TaD par l’Ecole Central de Lyon (AVENUE) et CARNET (MultiDEPART) et son transfert à 4 autorités publiques, en Slovénie (PO), au Portugal (TML), en Italie (ALOT), en Grèce (PF) et au réseau d’iED. ArtMED aboutira à la production de : 4 déclarations d’intention au niveau politique, 4 plans d’investissement et 2 plans de transport pour déployer les TaD.

Les TaD étant très peu développés dans la zone Euro-MED, ArtMED est le premier projet à compiler les connaissances existantes en un outil de planification à destination des décideurs publics pour offrir une solution de transport public aux populations des zones faiblement peuplées en Euro-MED.

en.name: Planning for autonomous mobility on demand in the Euro-MED area
en.description:
In Euro-MED, 40.6 million people live in sparsely populated areas with poor or without public transport services (IEMED, 2021). These areas are expensive for Public (Transport) Authorities (PTAs) to operate due to low number of paying passengers per kilometre. This leads to high private car dependency or isolation of people (ESPON, 2020), resulting in high carbon emissions (156M tons of CO2) or inaccessibility to public services and labour market.

A solution recognised by researchers and pilot projects in Europe is autonomous mobility on demand (AMOD), offering higher quality services while reducing operating costs (41% quality-cost ratio improvement, Malmö Uni, 2022) and CO2 emissions up to 72% (K2Centrum, 2022). However, it is difficult for PTAs to start planning AMOD without the ability to easily estimate its potential socio-economic and environmental impact.

The AVENUE (H2020) and MultiDEPART (EIT UM) projects successfully developed methodologies to assess local impact of AMOD. Currently, these methodologies cannot easily be applied by PTAs outside of these projects. Therefore, the primary objective of ArtMED is enable PTAs to plan for AMOD by transferring methodologies from École Centrale de Lyon (AVENUE) and CARNET (MultiDEPART) to 4 PTAs in Slovenia (PO), Portugal (TML), Italy (ALOT), Greece (PF) and beyond through jointly optimising and implementing a publicly accessible AMOD impact assessment tool in a transnational approach.

The tool will support PTAs in ArtMED to analyse the potential AMOD impact for local use cases and develop: 4 vision statements (for political support), 4 investment plans (for budgeting) and 2 transport model designs (for planning AMOD systems).

As AMOD hardly exists yet in Euro-MED, ArtMED is the first project to make state-of-the-art insights available to PTAs through a user-friendly tool, that assesses AMOD impact to plan for more accessible, inclusive, and sustainable public transport for people in sparsely populated areas.
en.expected_achievements:
To optimise and publish a web-based, user-friendly, Euro-MED tailored tool that allows public (transport) authorities (PTAs) in EuroMED to easily assess the sustainability and socio-economic impact of AMOD in their local areas.
The ArtMED public (transport) authorities learn how the AMOD impact assessment tool can be used to develop 1) vision statements, 2) investment plans and 3) Transport model designs to plan for AMOD deployment in their local regions.
To have 30 Euro-MED PTAs use the publshed AMOD impact assessment tool
To make 50 high-level Euro-MED decision makers (PTA directors, politicians) aware of the potential impact of AMOD to make public transport more sustainable, inclusive and equitable
en.actual_achievements:
The ArtMED project is dedicated to exploring and advancing Automated Mobility on Demand (AMOD) in the Euro-MED area. The project has made significant progress, focusing on developing best practices, creating comprehensive vision statements, and crafting tools for impact assessment and implementation.
In the 1st reporting period we reached the objectives as follows:

Best Practices Collection (WP1):

We produced a comprehensive document detailing the levels of flexibility and stages of AMOD development across different regions, including state-of-the-art examples of automated and on-demand mobility, with examples of successful implementations. In this document, we also analysed drivers and barriers for the implementation of AMOD in public transport.

Vision Statements (WP2):

We started developing detailed Vision Statements outlining the relevance, potential impact, and feasible deployment timelines for AMOD in partners specific use cases. Then, these documents were validated by an internal version of the impact assessment tool to enhance the final version of the partners' Vision Statements. 

Tool Development (WP1):

We defined a list of inputs and outputs for the AMOD impact assessment tool, and integrated methodologies from AVENUE and MultiDEPART projects to optimise the tool. By doing so, we are developing a flexible tool for contextualising service scenarios, assessing social relevance, and creating implementation roadmaps. The working version of the tool was sent to all the partners for testing and is currently awaiting feedback for further development.

Promotional Material and Outreach (WP3):

We finalized the project website and maintained an updated the to-do list with direct deliverables. We also developed a PowerPoint template, a Branding Manual, a LinkedIn page, and a Report template.

Main Outputs Delivered:
Deliverable 1.1.6 - AMOD Best Practices Collection:
We completed a detailed document covering the stages of AMOD development, state-of-the-art examples, drivers, barriers, and executive summaries 

Added-Value of the Cooperation in the 1st reporting periode:
Interdisciplinary Collaboration:
The project fosters interdisciplinary collaboration, bringing together experts from various fields to enrich the development of AMOD solutions. This cooperative approach ensures diverse perspectives are considered, leading to more robust and adaptable outcomes.

Knowledge Sharing:
Partners actively share feedback and experiences, leveraging each other's work to enhance the project's overall quality. This exchange of knowledge is crucial for developing well-rounded and effective AMOD strategies.

Stakeholder Engagement:
Engaging a wide range of stakeholders, from local municipalities to technology providers, ensures that the developed solutions are practical, scalable, and aligned with real-world needs. This engagement is pivotal for the successful implementation of AMOD in the Euro-MED area.

In the 1st reporting period, the ArtMED project has made substantial progress towards its goal of advancing Automated Mobility on Demand in the Euro-MED area. Through the comprehensive Best Practices Collection, detailed Vision Statements, and the development of robust tools, the project is paving a strong foundation for the future implementation of AMOD solutions. The added value of interdisciplinary cooperation and stakeholder engagement has been crutial in achieving these milestones, ensuring that the project's outcomes are both innovative and practical.
During the reporting period, the ARTMED Consortium engaged in a series of virtual and in-person meetings, including four conference calls, to ensure alignment on key project deliverables and to address any concerns related to the modification request. These calls were vital in keeping all partners up-to-date on progress, discussing the next steps, and resolving any challenges that arose. The calls also provided an opportunity to review timelines, share updates from working groups, and assess any modifications or adjustments required to meet project goals.
In addition to the conference calls, an important milestone was two in-person meetings held in Palaio Faliro, Greece, October, and Lyon, France, in February 2025. The face-to-face interaction allowed for more dynamic exchanges on specific project components, facilitating a clearer understanding of any technical and administrative questions in response to the evolving nature of the project. It also enabled members to reinforce their commitment to the project’s objectives and ensure that all required tasks were being addressed in the most effective manner.
A significant event during this period was the participation in the Green Living Areas (GLA) Mission 1st Institutional Policy Dialogue held on 10 January 2024. The dialogue served as an essential platform for engaging with institutional stakeholders, policymakers, and experts on the project’s goals and policy implications. Further contributing to the ongoing discourse on green living areas, the consortium also participated in the Green Living Areas Mission Thematic Working Group Online Sessions, held on 24 December 2024. These online sessions provided a space for ongoing projects  and external stakeholders to exchange ideas and best practices on key thematic areas related to green living spaces, sustainability, and urban resilience. The next major online event, that project partners attended, within GLA Online Thematic Working Groups focusing on policy, scheduled for Thursday, 27 February 2025. 
In terms of deliverables, during this reporting period, the consortium successfully completed and submitted four investment plans. These plans are integral to the project’s ability to attract funding and investment, ensuring that the necessary resources are in place for implementation. Each investment plan was meticulously developed, incorporating both short-term and long-term financial considerations, and ensuring that the project’s goals could be met within the agreed timeline. Alongside the investment plans, four vision statements were finalized, reflecting the consortium’s aspirations for the ArtMED project and its long-term impact. These vision statements outline the project’s overarching goals, including the creation of sustainable urban spaces, the integration of green technologies, and the promotion of resilient infrastructure. They also highlight the importance of community engagement, policy alignment, and the fostering of partnerships among local, national, and international stakeholders.
Furthermore, the consortium made significant progress in the development of the AMOD tool. A working version of the tool was produced during this period, marking a key achievement in the project’s technological component. The AMOD tool is designed to support decision-making processes and provide a robust framework for evaluating the environmental, social, and economic impacts of various interventions. This tool has been essential in the creation of the four investment plans and the four vision statements. By utilizing the AMOD tool, the consortium was able to ensure that these plans and statements were well-informed, evidence-based, and aligned with the project's goals. The tool's ability to integrate and analyze complex data played a pivotal role in shaping the strategic direction of the project, facilitating more informed decisions that will contribute to its long-term success.

fr.name: SustainabiLIty of tourism By EnhaCing Cooperation and dIgital transformatiOn
fr.description:
Le tourisme est l'un des moteurs de l'économie méditerranéenne et pionnier des technologies de transformation numérique.
Covid-19 l'a sévèrement frappé, montrant sa vulnérabilité et sa non-résilience aux risques imprévisibles et la rigidité de la gouvernance pour y réagir et les atténuer, puis exiger plus d'informations au temps opportun pour les politiques et les décideurs.
LIBECCIO fait face à ce défi en améliorant la gouvernance touristique, la gestion des destinations puis la résilience face aux risques.
L'utilisation des technologies de transformation numérique pour un paradigme touristique plus efficace, durable et résilient vise à fournir aux décideurs politiques une prise de décision éclairée basée sur les données et un impact positif sur la mise en œuvre des politiques.
LIBECCIO suit une approche intersectorielle et transdisciplinaire en Euro Med en impliquant la communauté touristique Q Helix dans la Plateforme d'Innovation Touristique Euro Med (ETIP) pour l'expérimentation de solutions adaptées suivant une méthodologie Living Lab.
Destination Management Support System (DMSS) est une plate-forme Web sémantiquement enrichie qui intègre des indicateurs de durabilité et des analyses de Big Data pour exploiter la prise de politiques et décision basées sur les données.
Le test DMSS dans les thématiques touristiques et les enseignements tirés sont évalués par l'ETIP qui élabore un plan de capitalisation sur le transfert et l'opérationnalisation des plateformes ETIP et DMSS dans les écosystèmes locaux, après la fin du Projet.
LIBECCIO bénéficie à ETIP de capacités institutionnelles communautaires, de solutions, d'outils, de méthodologies et de politiques.
Il capitalise les résultats des projets précédents en dotant les administrateurs publics régionaux et locaux et les OSA de solutions innovantes, basées sur des indicateurs de durabilité et le Big Data en temps réel, permettant une analyse comparative et une prise de décision au temps opportun.
fr.expected_achievements:
Methodological approach for up-scaling a data driven Destination Management Support System, improving visitors Big Data and destination sustainability Indicators collection and Big Data Analytics of capitalized tools.
Pilot testing of data driven tourism analysis and benchmarking approach for short / medium / long term sustainability decision making support, by up-scaling the Destination Management Support System and setting up and activating local Living Labs.
Establishment of the EuroMed tourist QHelix transnational community to promote sustainable tourism and develop a capitalization plan on how to make its DMSS tool operational in partner ecosystems after the end of the project
fr.actual_achievements:
During the 3rd RP, the LIBECCIO consortium made substantial and well-coordinated progress across all work packages, combining technical development, methodological design, stakeholder engagement, and communication activities. The period was marked by both consolidation of earlier achievements and preparation for the pilot testing phase of the project. A central focus was the advancement of the DMSS. Several partners contributed with expertise, data, and methodological insights to refine the platform’s architecture and functionalities. ATHENA (PP3) concentrated on data acquisition processes and visualization tools for stakeholders such as policy makers and enterprises, while RDA(PP7) and RAIS(PP9) provided inputs on indicators, data availability, and system testing. The RWG (PP10) and ERR (LP) helped coordinate the technical discussions and the integration of institutional perspectives.Together, these contributions ensured that the DMSS moved from conceptual requirements toward a more concrete, test-ready tool. Closely linked to this was the development of the Living Lab methodology, which will guide the testing of the DMSS in real-world contexts.CNA Abruzzo (PP2) took a leading role by drafting and presenting the initial methodology at the Patras consortium meeting. This emphasized a participatory approach, where local authorities, tourism operators, SMEs, and research institutions collaborate to validate and adapt services dynamically, using real data and seasonal variations.Other partners enriched the methodology with contextual feedback, ensuring that it would be adaptable across diverse territories.By the end of the period, the methodology had been collectively refined and finalized, creating a shared operational roadmap for the next phase. Parallel to the technical and methodological work, the consortium devoted significant energy to stakeholder engagement and communication. A series of Info Days were organized in multiple locations (Patras, Terrassa, Nessebar, Kotor, East Sarajevo, Aigio, Agrinio, Rimini, and Cesenatico) reaching audiences that included municipal and regional authorities, tourism businesses, universities, NGOs, cultural institutions, and citizens.These events played a dual role: they raised awareness about the LIBECCIO project and its objectives and promoted the (ETIP), inviting stakeholders to join and contribute data for DMSS testing.The CCIT (PP5) consolidated the reports of all Info Days, while local partners tailored their events to the specific needs and dynamics of their territories. Beyond local outreach, LIBECCIO strengthened its visibility and networking at the Euro-MED level, through the participation of PP in thematic seminars and international events (D4T in Brussels, CircletheMed in Athens, and EuroMed cluster innovation workshops).These activities enabled exchanges with other projects, opening the way for synergies, joint actions, and shared learning within the broader community. Communication activities intensified: social media accounts were launched or strengthened, newsletters were circulated, and a promotional video was produced by the Lead Partner. Local and national media were engaged, ensuring broader dissemination of project results. In summary, the third reporting period consolidated LIBECCIO’s technical, methodological, and communication activities. The consortium advanced on the DMSS design and the Living Lab methodology, but also ensured that the project’s vision was rooted in local territories, supported by engaged stakeholders, and connected to Euro-MED networks. This integrated approach reinforced LIBECCIO’s positioning as a driver of innovation in sustainable and data-driven tourism management across the Mediterranean.
During the 3rd RP, two deliverables were successfully finalized, while two others remain under development. The Info Day Report (D.3.1.1), originally scheduled for Period 2, was completed in this reporting period. The report documents the organization of 22 Info Days held across partner regions, fully aligned with LIBECCIO’s communication, dissemination, and stakeholder engagement strategy. en.name: SustainabiLIty of tourism By EnhaCing Cooperation and dIgital transformatiOn
en.description:
Tourism is one of the driver of the global economy and an early adopter of digital transformation technologies in Mediterranean basin.
Covid-19 pandemic hit severely, showing vulnerability and non-resilience to unpredictable risks and governance rigidity in reacting and mitigating them then finally demanding more timely information for policy and decision makers.
LIBECCIO faces this challenge enhancing tourism governance, destination management and sustainability then resilience against risks too. Through digital transformation, it addresses the need for improved destination management, supporting policy makers with informed data driven decision making, positively impacting the policies implementation for a more sustainable tourism paradigm.
It follows an intersectoral and transdisciplinary approach in Euro Med by involving together with partners the Q Helix tourism community, through the Euro Med Tourism Innovation Platform (ETIP), in testing and transfer planning of suitable solutions following a Living Lab methodology.
LIBECCIO Destination Management Support System (DMSS) is a semantically enriched web-enabled platform that integrates sustainability indicator datasets and Big Data analytics to exploit data driven policy and decision making. The DMSS test in thematic tourism partner areas and the lessons learned are assessed and evaluated by ETIP that draws up a capitalization plan how the partners will transfer and make operational in local ecosystems the ETIP platform and its main tool DMSS after the end of the Project. 
LIBECCIO benefits ETIP with community institutional capacities, through solutions, methodologies and policies for a more efficient, sustainable and resilient tourism paradigm.
It capitalizes previous projects outcomes equipping regional and local public administrators and BSOs with innovative solutions, integrating tourist sustainability indicators and real time Big Data, enabling timely analysis, advanced benchmarking and decision making.
en.expected_achievements:
Methodological approach for up-scaling a data driven Destination Management Support System, improving visitors Big Data and destination sustainability Indicators collection and Big Data Analytics of capitalized tools.
Pilot testing of data driven tourism analysis and benchmarking approach for short / medium / long term sustainability decision making support, by up-scaling the Destination Management Support System and setting up and activating local Living Labs.
Establishment of the EuroMed tourist QHelix transnational community to promote sustainable tourism and develop a capitalization plan on how to make its DMSS tool operational in partner ecosystems after the end of the project

en.name: Dialogue4Nature, the Institutional Dialogue Project of the Interreg Euro-MED Natural Heritage Mission
en.description:
Mediterranean society is dependent on natural resources, being densely populated and one of the world’s most visited areas. The basin is a biodiversity hotspot hosting 18% of the world’s marine biodiversity with high levels of endemism. It is also a climate hotspot. Chronic climate change is heating the Mediterranean Sea three times faster than the global mean, with changes to biodiversity, habitats, migration of endemic species and invasion of non-indigenous species. Intensifying acute extreme events result in storm surges, heat waves, floods, and droughts. The current decline of biodiversity is unsustainable, loss must be halted, and society must learn to adapt and mitigate climate change.
The governance of the Mediterranean is fragmented. Many regional challenges are poorly identified and addressed through ad hoc policy measures at local, regional, or national level. This project will take best practices from the associated thematic projects and upscale to Mediterranean and EU policy level, engaging with decision makers and relevant authorities at the science-policy interface. In addition, through the global networks of the partners results will be showcased at the international level.
Inspired by successful policy initiatives such as the Stockholm resilience centre and the iberoamerican network of climate change, the Dialogue4Nature project will establish a Mediterranean Resilience Network (MRN). This network will coordinate cooperation at the broader Mediterranean scale on climate change, biodiversity, and sustainable development policies. Dialogue4Nature project will foster cooperation and to promote a more participatory and integrated policy process for climate change and biodiversity, including sub-national and civil society. In this manner, natural capital will be mainstreamed into social and environmental policy, not only helping to protect the environment but also to improve people’s health and well-being and to ensure a more resilient socio-economic model.
en.expected_achievements:
To enable conditions for the exploitation and re-use of governance knowledge, experiences and results from thematic projects  by relevant multilevel stakeholders throughout the Mediterranean region 
Encourage and foster the transfer of nature practices and results to other actors and territories and their integration  in the development of local, regional, national, european and international nature policies and strategies. 


To set up a permanent Mediterranean Resilience Network (MRN) between relevant institutions, organisations, and stakeholders to integrate nature, climate change and food security into policy making at transnational Mediterranean level

fr.name: Dialogues sur la gouvernance pour une Méditerranée résiliente
fr.description:
La Méditerranée, étant densément peuplée et l'une des régions les plus visitées au monde, dépend des ressources naturelles. Le bassin méditerranéen est un point chaud de biodiversité, abritant 18% de la biodiversité marine mondiale avec des niveaux élevés d'endémisme. C'est aussi un point chaud climatique : la mer Méditerranée se réchauffe trois fois plus vite que la moyenne mondiale, avec des changements dans la biodiversité, les habitats, la migration d'espèces endémiques et l'invasion d'espèces non indigènes. Le déclin actuel n'est pas durable, la perte doit être arrêtée et la société doit apprendre à s'adapter et à atténuer le changement climatique.

La gouvernance en Méditerranée est fragmentée. De nombreux défis régionaux sont mal identifiés et abordés par le biais de mesures politiques ad hoc au niveau local, régional ou national. Ce projet reprendra les meilleures pratiques des projets thématiques associés pour atteindre les politiques méditerranéennes et de l’UE, en s'engageant avec des décideurs politiques et des autorités compétentes à l'interface science-politique. De plus, grâce aux réseaux mondiaux des partenaires, les résultats seront présentés au niveau international.

Inspiré par des initiatives politiques réussies telles que le centre de résilience de Stockholm et le réseau ibéro-américain sur le changement climatique, le projet Go4Med Nature établira un réseau méditerranéen de résilience (RMR). Ce réseau coordonnera la coopération à l'échelle méditerranéenne en matière de changement climatique, biodiversité et politiques de développement durable. Le projet Go4Med Nature favorisera la collaboration et encouragera un processus politique plus participatif et intégré. De cette manière, le capital naturel sera intégré dans la politique sociale et environnementale, contribuant non seulement à protéger l'environnement, mais aussi à améliorer la santé et le bien-être des personnes et à garantir un modèle socio-économique plus résilient.

en.name: C4Nature
en.description:
Med regions share common challenges related to global change impacts: environmental degradation, biodiversity loss & lack of appropriate governance mechanisms. C4Nature aims to achieve coordination -among different levels- of knowledge, best practices & policies to approach the challenges of biodiversity protection and climate change mitigation/adaptation. It enhances the coordination between multi-level stakeholders such as public authorities, sectoral agencies, schools, academic & research centers, NGOs, etc. in the Med area. Concretely, C4Nature will establish a Community of Practice and deliver an Adaptive Resource recipient, a Methodology for assessing the potential of available community "resources", a set of training programmes, a portfolio of services embedded in the Community Amplification Room including a Mentorship programme addressed to specific target groups.

In the Mediterranean, the current fragmentation of institutional, financial, and political arenas, as well as a lack of coordinated support, will be overcome by identifying opportunities that benefit all parties, boosting common interests and tackling current inertia towards participation in collaborative environments. Transnational cooperation is a key enabler for capitalising lessons learned from pilot cases and successful policy implementation results.

The real novelties of C4Nature are:
i) the "tailor- made approach" that applies to all planned activities and 
ii) the availability of an initial "core community" which allows a smooth identification and engagement of the target groups.

According to the coupled philosophy of "resource valorization", C4Nature always exploits, reuses, reshapes the available knowledge, the existing practices and infrastructure (produced by Euro-MED thematic and strategic projects) to avoid the duplication of efforts and to amplify the results.
en.expected_achievements:
Facilitating the re-use of knowledge/practices by TPs by establishing a community of practice.Building the conditions for identifying transferable results & concrete solutions for climate change mitigation/adaptation & protection of natural resources
Facilitating the transfer of evidence-based results from TPs to other stakeholders/territories.
Empowering the transfer abilities of the core community for uptaking solutions.
Supporting mainstreaming into local,regional,national policies & practices
Increasing the coordination and systematic exchange of knowledge and practices among actors of the Mediterranean region and beyond (multi-level, transnational) to accelerate the implementation of activities addressed to achieve Mission 2 objectives.

During the first year of activities, the partners spend a large part of the first 6 months for coordinating the technical team (WPs responsible partners) for finalizing the design of the overall approach and building the roadmaps for the three WPs (SHARING/REUSE, TRANSFER/MAINSTREAM, COORDINATION), while effort was put on communication activities aiming to give visibility to the project and the Natural Heritage Mission as a whole. At the end of the year an expert communication manager, for boosting the social media channels. Expecially after the project meeting in Barcelona that took place at the end of the first semester, the project launched some of its activities, such as the community building set-up, the stakeholders mapping, the resources’ collection, the first awareness campaign, the design of the Academy and the forming of the amplification room and its respective Working Groups. The basis of the cooperation with the Dialogue4Nature (D4N) project was also set, as well as relationships with the other Interreg Euro-MED Missions and other Initiatives and institutions (e.g. UfM, EC, etc).
As for WP1 – SHARING/REUSE, development of the ‘REUSE Roadmap’, overviewing the WP1 activities, as well as the foreseen synergies with the D4N project (Activity 1.1 REUSE roadmap and WP coordination). In this context, many meetings with the project’s partners were held, as well as with the D4N partners coordinating D4N’s relevant activities. All partners participated in the preparation and implementation of the two community building events and one community building activity (Activity 1.2 Community of practice set-up), i.e., the KoM of Community4Nature (22 March 2023) with the participation of Associated Partners (APs), the hybrid event held in Barcelona (9 June 2023), co-organised with D4N, and engaging the Mission’s APs, and the community building session during the first Institutional Dialogue event (D4N activity) in Piran (Slovenia). All partners, duly coordinated, contributed in the development and updating of the excel tool aiming to map the Mission’s stakeholders (Activity 1.3 Mapping of stakeholders and potential target groups). One of the first activities to be activated has been the methodology for resources collection and the draft Adaptive Resources Recipient (Activity 1.4). All partners contributed by adding resources to the Adaptive Resources Recipient. Moreover, a great effort has been done by the communication team in designing and implementing the awareness campaign on coastal erosion (Activity 1.9 “SOLVE the CHALLENGE” campaigns). 

In WP2, leaded by the WP responsible, all partners contributed to the development of the ‘TRANSFER roadmap’ (Activity 2.1 TRANSFER roadmap and WP coordination). As for the Interreg Euro-MED Academy activities (Activity 2.2.)  during the second period the partnership focused on the design of the first summer school in presence. A discussions for the design of the services of the Amplification Room (Activity 2.3 Creation of the C4Nature Community Amplification Room) has been initiated, while all partners actively contributed to the launching of the thematic Working Groups (WGs) and the elaboration of the relevant factsheets (Activity 2.5 Transfer by clustering through WGs for strategic liaising, exchanges and advice panels). In addition, partners took part in Activity 2.6 (Mainstreaming support to D4N), where contribution to the policy related objectives was offered to D4N, mainly on the organisation of the first Institutional Dialogue event on 22 & 23 November 2023, in Slovenia. Finally, some partners are actively involved in the Mission4Nature joint communication team that meets every week for planning and implementing the planned communication activities (Activity 2.7 Communication and dissemination). 

In WP3, all partners contributed to the overall project’s planning. In the first semester a restricted group has participated in the elaboration of the application for receiving the EU Green Week and EMD In my Country labelling for specific project activities, in the context of Activity 3.3 Synergies with other programmes and initiatives, as well as to the contribution/co-organization of several online events with the Union for the Mediterranean. Finally, many partners and especially the LP and WPs responsible participated in meetings with JS representatives and the other three Missions regarding the Programme’s annual event held in Slovenia on 21 & 22 November 2023 (Activity 3.4 Coordination among governance projects and with JS). Specifically C4N played a relevant role by coordinating the inter-mission team that co-organized the presentation of the old transversal horizontal projects of the previous programme period and the governance framework of the new one. Moreover it took care of the session dedicated to the young voices and produced an introductory video for the event.

WP1
During the first semester of this second year of activities, this WP has been implemented with the contribution of all partners. In specific, all partners participated in the design, organisation and implementation of the community building events and activities (main objective: welcoming the TPs including them in the community – 26th January 1st online meeting with LPs, and in presence in July next period) one partner has taken in charge the relation with 2 or more projects. As for the development and updating of the database of target groups and stakeholders a decision has been taken: its functionality must be improved through the design and implementation of a new tailor-made CRM tool. IrRADIARE will work on this task. As for the the resources collection (whose methodology needs to be integrated and slightly revised), it has been further implemented. Moreover the communication team has worked for the planning and the development of the first contents for the raise awareness campaign on restoration (launched in Piran in June).
WP2
This WP has been framed by CREAF in its complex mechanism during this period and some activities have been already initiated. The revision of the roadmap and strategy of this WP has been developed during the joint meetings with D4N as many activities are synergic. During this period the partners also started the activities related to the Interreg Euro-MED Academy, i.e., whose first step is the organization of a first in-presence summer school on Restoration in Iran the 18-19 1nd 20 June. A part of this activity will be included as a session in the upcoming online Academy (activity to be coordinated at programme level - Activity 2.2 Contribution to the Interreg Euro-MED Academy). All partners were also involved in the development of the Working Groups activities (initiated by D4N) the 26 of April. The communication team goes on with the weekly meetings for implementing the Editorial plan at monthly basis. 
WP3
The most important activity in this WP is the  COORDINATION relation with D4N, the governance projects and JS. As for the D$N synergic working plan, the partners meet at least once a moth or even more when urgent issues needs to be finalized. In addition, during this period the partnes of the Mission worked hardly for preparing 2 activities that have been included in EMD and Eu Green week in June/July. Synergies with other programmes and initiatives are implemented whenever possible. Finally, all partners participated in joint meetings with D4N, TPs and APS both online and in presence. 
One important activity during the second half of 2024 was the organization of the 2nd Institutional Dialogue event, where all C4N partners participated, expecially in the organisation of the 2nd Day (community building session, including a “geo-clustering” exercise).
Another important achievement is the design and testing of an online tool (CRM) to better manage the Mission’s targeted stakeholders. This online tool will substitute the excel we used till now. [Activity 1.3 - Mapping of stakeholders and potential target groups]. 
During this period, the Peer-review methodology was elaborated in its draft version by the responsible partner and all partners contributed and addressed the methodology. It explains the process of collecting, analysing and tailoring the TPs results in order to be reused/transferred. Many partners (expecially the WP responsible partners participated to the specific Working Groups creared by the JS for setting up the Library and the EuroMed Academy.
All partners (expecially those involved in the communication team) contributed to the implementation of the 2024 awareness campaign on nature restoration, organising also 6 National Info Days i [Activity 1.9 - Solve the Challenge campaigns and Activity 2.7 - Communication and dissemination].
Another important activity that took place during this semester is the drafting of the methodology/scheme explaining the ‘transfer through ownership approach and community empowerment’ [Activity 2.4] and to the preparation of 
the clustering approach.
The coordination activity with D4N went on smoothly as in the previous periods improving the interaction quality and effectiveness [Activity 2.6 - Mainstreaming support to D4N]. It collaborated with D4N, in the context of MED Talks 2024 online event (organiser UfM, co-organiser Interreg Euro-MED Programme, 16.12.2024), where a focused session on ecosystem restoration and biodiversity conservation in the Mediterranean was presented [Activity 3.3 - Synergies with other programmes and initiatives].
C4N participated in 2 inter-Missions and Euro-MED4Governance online meetings: the 2nd Governance Community Projects meeting (21.10.2024) and the Euro-MED4Governance group meeting (13.11.2024). [Activity 3.4 - Coordination among governance projects and with JS].
During this semester the communication team decided to launch a coordination with the TPs CMs. So the 1st online joint communication meeting with TPs communication officers took place on 10.12.2024. [Activity 2.7 - Communication and dissemination].
All partners participated in the design, organisation and implementation of the community building events and activities, in the development and updating of the database of target groups and stakeholders whose functionality will be improved through the design and implementation of a new tailor-made CRM tool, supported the resources collection (whose methodology needs to be integrated and slightly revised),  the implementation and development of content for the raise awareness campaign on restoration (launched in Piran in June).
As for the WP2, it has been framed in its complex mechanism during this period and some activities have been already initiated. The partners followed also some activities related to the Interreg Euro-MED Academy, i.e., whose first step has been the organization of a first in-presence summer school on Restoration in Piran the 18-19 1nd 20 June. A part of this activity will be included as a session in the upcoming online Academy (activity to be coordinated at programme level)  (Activity 2.2 Contribution to the Interreg Euro-MED Academy). 
C4N partners were involved in the development of the Working Groups activities (coordinated by D4N).
Two activities of the project have been framed in the EMD in my country and in the Eu Green week initiatives in June and July (in the context of Piran and Rovinj in presence meetings). Synergies with other programmes and initiatives are implemented whenever possible. 

Main achievements in WP1 during this period:
A1.2 - C4N participated in TPs meetings and events. In May, C4N welcomed online (individually) the 3 new TPs (INCORE-MED, MIRAMAR and RENFORCE). Preparation of the next Community of Practice meeting in Athens (2nd and 3rd of July).  
A1.3 - The new CRM tool (CERES) for mapping the NHM stakeholders was finalised. To be presented in the next Euro-MED4Governance Group meeting. 
A1.5 - Peer review methodology was established and the process kicked off for most important outputs and deliverables for each TP.
A1.7 - In April, C4N participated with D4N in the United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE) Regional Forum on Sustainable Development, co-organising the webinar “Integrating Biodiversity and Governance: Building a Mission for Sustainable Development”.
A1.9 - Preparation of the 2025 Solve the Challenge awareness campaign on environmental governance. To be launched on the 2nd of July during the Community of Practice meeting in Athens.

Main achievements in WP2 during this period:
Activity 2.3: Amplification Room dynamics were established through bilateral meetings with each TP, reaching an agreement on the most suitable activities of the portfolio of services to be put in place (A2.4, A2.5). 
For Activity 2.2: MOOC modules were identified and TPs engaged in developing proper contributions, in coordination with JS specific working groups. 
Activity 2.6: OFB engagement reestablished. 

Main achievements in WP3 during this period:
Activity 3.1 - main achievement was the work developed on the update on the Joint Work Plan of 2025-2026 of C4N and D4N. 
On Activity 3.2: all partners are continuously updating and considering the carbon footprint tool and methodology established. 
Under the scope of Activity 3.3 and 3.4 the preparation and submission of a stand and event proposal was developed on the framework of European Week of Regions 2025. This was done  in close coordination with the EuroMED4Governance group and the JS. The event mentioned in A1.7 also allowed for synergies with other programmes and initiatives (A 3.3). Synergies (A3.3) were also promoted through the participation of C4N on Interact Webinars: Interreg for a Greener Europe webinar series | Disaster risk management with a roundtable contribution and promotion of LocAll4Flood Thematic Project and also Interreg GO! Event contributing to a roundtable on Water Resilience Strategy and importance of NHM to conserve and protect natural heritage

UN OCEAN conference in Nice on the 10th and 11th of June / two events organised: (a) Scaling up Posidonia Conservation: Insights from Practice and Policy (with ARTEMIS and the Mediterranean Posidonia Network) and (b) Bridging Natural Heritage and Sustainable Tourism: Euro-MED Perspectives on Marine and Coastal Policies (with Sustainable Tourism Mission).

fr.description:
Les régions Méd. partagent des défis communs liés aux impacts du changement global: dégradation de l'environnement, perte de biodiversité & manque de mécanismes de gouvernance appropriés. C4Nature vise à assurer la coordination -entre différents niveaux- de connaissance, meilleures pratiques et politiques pour aborder les défis de la protection de la biodiversité & de l'atténuation du changement climatique. Il améliore la coordination entre des multi-niveaux acteurs dans la Méd, tels que autorités publiques, agences sectorielles, écoles, centres de recherche, ONG, etc. Concrètement, C4Nature établira une Communauté de Pratique et fournira un récipient de Ressource Adaptative, une Méthodologie pour évaluer le potentiel des «ressources» disponibles de la Communauté, un ensemble de programmes de formation, un portefeuille de services intégrés dans la Salle de la Communauté d'Amplification, y compris un programme de Mentorat adressé à des groupes cibles spécifiques.
En Méd, la fragmentation institutionnelle, financière et politique, ainsi que le manque de soutien coordonné, seront surmontés en identifiant les opportunités pour le profit de toutes parties, en renforçant les intérêts communs et en s'attaquant à l'inertie actuelle vis-à-vis de la participation dans des environnements collaboratifs. La coopération transnationale est un catalyseur clé pour capitaliser les enseignements tirés par les cas pilotes et les résultats réussis de la mise en œuvre des politiques.
La vraie nouveauté du projet. est:
i) "l'approche sur mesure" qui s'applique à toutes les activités prévues, et
ii) la disponibilité d'une «communauté de base» initiale qui permet l'identification et l'engagement des groupes cibles.
Selon la philosophie couplée de «valorisation de ressources», C4Nature exploite, réutilise, remodèle la connaissance disponible, les pratiques & les infrastructures existantes (produits par les projets Euro-MED) évitant la duplication des efforts & amplifiant les résultats.

Le manque de coopération entre les autorités publiques et les décideurs, le manque de dialogue multisectoriel entre les parties prenantes du tourisme en matière de durabilité, la saisonnalité, le changement climatique, le faible niveau de digitalisation, sont quelques-uns des principaux défis auxquels le secteur doit faire face. Ces défis peuvent être résolus, qu’en utilisant une approche intersectorielle, et qu’en créant des structures de dialogue innovantes et durables entre les décideurs.

L’objectif global du projet vise à créer un schéma de collaboration entre les parties prenantes du tourisme de la zone et au-delà (autorités politiques, universités, secteur privé…).
La multiplication des interdépendances entre les domaines d’action permet de créer des synergies et d’adopter une approche plus systémique pour définir et relever les défis. L’objectif est de renforcer la durabilité des politiques touristiques en améliorant le niveau de coordination et la capacité institutionnelle des autorités publiques, et en atteignant les organismes, les programmes, les stratégies et initiatives de plusieurs niveaux.
Le projet de dialogue institutionnel travaillera main dans la main avec le projet de communauté thématique de la mission 4, en participant au travail visant à guider les projets thématiques dans la conception de stratégies pouvant produire des recommandations politiques transférables.
D’une part GOV4MED co-construira, avec l’ensemble de la communauté du tourisme durable, des résultats, des stratégies et des politiques publiques transférables, et, d’autre part il garantira que ces résultats atteignent d’importantes parties prenantes et institutions décisionnelles afin qu’ils soient intégrés dans les politiques régionales, nationales et internationales.
L’ambition est de diffuser la vision unique du Programme, un tourisme équitable et responsable pour tous, dans plus de territoires, dans plus d’organes décisionnels, en façonnant l’avenir du tourisme dans la zone.
fr.actual_achievements:
We have devoted this first period to create a global action strategy and project procedures for a multilevel and paralel coordination in different axes: internal of the partnership, coordination with the JS, coordination in the framework of the Sustainable Tourism Mission together with C4T Project, coordination with the PAs and coordination with external actors to ensure transferability and mainstreaming in the whole future process, as is the case of UfM and the contacts initiated with Unit 3 of DG Mare. We believe that it is important from the beginning to articulate a permanent structure of institutional dialogue and collaboration within different governance levels and different territories, to build up collaboration mechanisms and models, synergies and to build capacity to policy-makers to serve this goal, alongside advocacy activities.
In addition, in order to take further steps in this period, the internal Statutes and Rules of Procedure of the project have been signed by all partners immediately after the signature of the Subsidy Contract and the corresponding Partnership Agreement. 
We think that the most feasible way of acting was to spend the necessary time at the beginning of the project to develop the appropriate mechanisms such as the foreseen documents in the Application Form already delivered: MoU with C4T project, the work plan updated with the template provided by the JS, the first document made for the coordination of internal communication processes in common with C4T Project, the calendar of common actions of the Mission, the internal meetings of the project, the meetings together with C4T for coordination of the Mission and the meetings with the JS; D4T has provided with contributions and feedback in all meetings participating and have studied the best and most feasible way to carry out the actions taking into account the objectives, target groups and outputs to be achieved in the project, the suggestions made by the JS and the permanent open dialogue with C4T Project and external actors. In this sense, we reached the Southern Mediterranean stakeholders through the participation and presentation of the project in a first meeting with this policy level in the 4th UfM Working Group on Environment and Climate Action and the presentation of all Missions of Interreg Euro-MED to this 4th Working Group that took place in Sitges in 05-23. Also, in the collaboration with external actors, we must also highlight the participation of the project partners in the event "Towards a more sustainable tourism in the Mediterranean - 3 Interreg programmes joining forces" in Florence 05-23 where our partners participated in all sessions and did the wrap up of one of the pannels. Concerning this, we have begun to lay the groundwork to achieve the Output 1.1. "Collaborative networks reinforcing the dialogue on sustainable tourism" and to establish the basis for other deliverables as the "Cross Network Alliance for governance in sustainable tourism and related policy recommendations".
The organization of the KoM in Athens, in which all partners participated (1 and 2-03-23) and in which the first steps for the establishment of the internal operating strategy were taken, was very useful for the internal coordination of the partnership in an effective way. It has been remarkable as well the participation in the event of external actors belonging to the policy level as the representatives of the Ministry of Regional Development and EU Funds of the Republic of Croatia-Directorate for European Territorial Cooperation, participants in the WG5 of Panoramed on Sustainable Tourism, Roadmap & the toolbox for tourism governance in the previous programming period. One of the successful tools for policy level to be transferred and capitalized in the present period.
We should also highlight as a basis for our capitalization work in this project that the first step has been the initial mapping of policies related to Tourism Transformation & prioritization. This document is the basis for a first common understanding between C4T and D4T Projects on where to focus Reuse and Transfer activities to accelerate Tourism Transformation with the following objectives:
-Identification and clustering of givers/takers for collaborative networks.
- Identification, clustering and matching of "reusable" outputs for tourism policy transformation.

Finally, and for the dialogue that we keep open in several directions, it is also key the activity of the project in BaseCamp -communication internal tool- providing contributions on topics both on contents and events as well as on implementation methodology requested by the JS and other Missions. 
Regarding the TGs to reach we have also started to reach different groups such as: National Public Authority, Regional Public Authority, International Organisation, Higher Education and Research Organization or Sectoral Agency, among others.


The works on the tool of A.1.1. "Joint mapping with C4T of D4T targeted policy priorities in Tourism" coordinated by IPTPO has continued. This key tool for governance in ST has been presented in the meeting of the Euro-MED Governance Group (Marseille, 2-3/07) (RP4).
We have also established the basis for the Workshop and Action Plan with C4T for policy-driven Reuse and Transferring (D.1.1.1.). IPTPO is coordinating this Action Plan and for this aim has organised two meetings online (27-02/ 27-05) with the active participation of partners involved in WP1.   
We presented the project with special emphasis to WP1 as a key work for the processes of transferring and mainstreaming to the policy level at the KoM of the ST Community in Rome (10-11/04). This action is linked to A.3.7. Communication for Coordination - involvement in key advocacy events & A.1.4. Communication for Reuse. Partners attending (LP, REGLAZ, IPTPO, REGSUD) participated at the MED Clusters. At the Cluster of Innovation, we raised a transferable tool: the system of indicators for Cultural Routes (Best MED Project) implemented by the CAST (Centre for Advanced Studies in ST) of Bologna's University.
As part of A.1.3. we participated in a meeting with C4T in this event in Rome for the update of the Joint Mapping and Action Plan in our purpose of involving the TPs of the ST Mission.
About  WP2, we have advanced in the internal planification through the follow up meetings to have exchanges on the key topics: 
-Preparation of the agenda of the SCM of the project organised by REGSUD that took place in Marseille (23/04) linked to A.1.3, A.2.6, and A.3.5  
-REGSUD organised several meetings together with involved partners of D4T and C4T for specifications of the future MOOC of the Euro-MED Academy. (A.2.5.)
-Meetings for the co-writing of the tender for the STPLs. (ASCAME + LP).
-Meetings for the coordination and roadmap of the NSTO. (LP+REGLAZ+GTI).
-Meeting for the participation in event in Varna focused on regional policies on ST -as one of our TGs- in the present year (LP+MV).
In addition to this,we are making progress in the following internal documents: Roadmap of the DTTPs (A.2.2) coordinated by ELA FPA; Roadmap of the Cross Network Alliance (D.3.3.2. / Output 3.1. coordinated by EPLO) and the Roadmap + tender of the STPLs (D.2.3.1. / Output 2.2. coordinated by ASCAME).
In terms of communication, we have co-written the ST Mission Joint Communication Strategy (01/24), focusing on the alignment with the RAS.
ASCAME participated at the 2nd MED Innovation Camp, organized by D4I Project in Barcelona. (A.2.6.) in a cross-Mission action.
On WP3, the “Circle the MED Forum, 7th edition” organised by EPLO in Athens (18-19/01) counted on the participation of the LP, NTOMNE and GTI (A.3.2/A.3.3/A.3.4 interlinked). The Forum achieved a high attendance with an online participation of 500 people from 45 countries and over 200 present attendees. 
ELA FPA participated as speaker in the round table entitled “Multilevel governance for circular & ST” (A.3.2.) moderated by EPLO. GTI moderated the round table “The circular economy-ST nexus in the MED, business models and monitoring starting from islands ” (D.3.2.1.) which highlights our commitment to key-advocacy events.
On the other hand, and for the official session in Marseille (4/04), we organised the “Round Table on the Cross-Network Alliance” (D.3.3.1) moderated by EPLO. With this aim, we established exchanges with DG Internal Market, Industry, and SMEs unit GROW G1 (EC), Urban Agenda-ST Partnership (EC), Urban Agenda Partnership on Innovative (EC), UfM, Interreg Next MED, and the NCP in France. We regard these contacts as a crucial step in engaging  the TGs and advancing towards the increasing of the institutional capacity of policymakers to enable mainstreaming and tourism policy transformations and the Output 3.1.
 
In A.3.5. ELA FPA together with the WPs’ leaders (EPLO + REGLAZ) and REGSUD meetings with C4T to harmonize deliverables and coordinate advocacy actions (incl. communication of Advocacy – A3.7), the Report of the JAF 2023 and the organization of the 2nd JAF of the ST Mission “Climate Adaptation in Tourism – Building capacity and policy integration” that will take place next 21/11. Organised as one of the side events within the Sun&Blue Congress, the event will contribute to the mitigation of climate change effects throughout the Med Tourism ecosystem.
Related to our contact with the JS, we have constant exchanges with our PO and other members of the JS for different topics. We are following closely the multi-programme coordination mechanism updates carried out by the JS to align it with the cross-network alliance for mainstreaming purposes. In this frame, all partners attended the internal SCM in Marseille (23/04) organised by REGSUD (interlinking A.1.3, A.2.6, and A.3.5) with the participation of the JS on the implementation of the activities and assistance on financial reporting.

en.name: Dialogue4Tourism. Institutional dialogue on Sustainable Tourism and Governance in the Euro-Med area
en.description:
Tourism urges to overcome a great range of challenges, including lack of solid cooperation among Euro-MED public authorities and policy makers, lack of multidisciplinary and multi sectoral dialogue of tourism related stakeholders for sustainability, overtourism, seasonality, climate change, crises like Covid-19, low level of digitalization, etc. 

To effectively address these challenges a shift of paradigm in governance -towards increased collaboration among levels, sectors and territories- is required. The pathway to tourism transformation requires increased capacities, institutional dialogue, improved and more effective collaboration and coordination mechanisms. Policy-makers are at the heart of the transition.

The overall objective of Dialogue4Tourism is to increase the coordination level and institutional capacity of public authorities, multi-level bodies, programmes, strategies and initiatives in the Euro-MED, aiming to transform tourism into greener, smarter and more resilient, embracing the fourfold approach of the mission: circularity of tourism services, environmental neutrality, sustainable ecosystem services, and cultural and natural preservation of resources. To achieve its goals, Dialogue4Tourism brings together 10 PPs and 25 APs and collaborates closely with Community4Tourism Project, the JS and the other Programme Missions.

Dialogue4Tourism establishes cooperation and advocacy mechanisms: Driving Tourism Transition Panel and Hub, Network of Sustainable Tourism Observatories deriving from BESTMED, the cross-synergies alliance and the Tourism Leaders Summit. Sustainable Tourism Policy Labs (STPLs) will be organised. A robust advocacy strategy will be deployed, to bring Mediterranean Tourism in the top of the policy-leaders agendas. Contribution to high-level and high profile events is ensured.

Dialogue4Tourism is expected to contribute to Mission#4 4 SOs, Green Deal, UfM Greener Med Agenda & Ministerial Declaration on Sustainable Blue Economy, Mediterranean Strategy for Sustainable Development etc.
en.expected_achievements:
To create the framework of collaboration with the Sustainable Tourism Community & the Programme’s Missions 1,2,3 Community4Tourism & Dialogue4Tourism so as to share & reuse knowledge produced having identified the policy making institutions that will receive that knowledge.
To fully exploit the “multi-SH Hub for Transforming MED Tourism” & NSTO so as to reinforce the transfer of re-usable results, mainstreaming them into policies. To increase capacity of recipients’ institutions & integrate results into their policies.
Increased coordination between policy making actors within and beyond Euro-MED area and across different governance levels and sectors. Joint advocacy activities involving in the dialogue EU policy making authorities and different programmes.

Moreover, we have directed our steps to continue consolidating the framework of collaboration with C4T Project since the Sustainable Tourism Community has been in the process of constitution once the Interreg Euro-MED Program announced the selected projects last November. The collaboration between both projects is being very fruitful and, in the frame of WP1, we requested the collaboration of the responsibles for WP1 of the C4T for feedback on the non key deliverable "Joint Map of targets for collaborative network that boost the impact of Reuse and Transfer". This document is important to reinforce the Output 1.1. and it is a “policy filter” of the forthcoming activities of the D4T and C4T projects.
In administrative terms, the partnership approved in written procedure the entrance of 2 new partners (Greening the Islands and ASCAME) with a split of the budget of the former partner that withdrew from the project. 
Regarding events and other activities, we celebrated the 1st SCM in Podgorica (Montenegro) that took place on 03/10/23. An update of all the WPs was made and the tasks of each partner and the outputs to be achieved were discussed. 
Subsequently, on 04/10/23, we celebrated the session on the Cross Network Alliance with the active participation of the representative of DG Mare (European Commission, Unit 3 Sea-Basin Strategies, Maritime Regional Cooperation & Maritime Security), Eleni Hatziyanni. The main lines of this event have been collected in D.3.3.1. "Annual Main Conclusions' paper on workshops and activities of the Cross-Network Alliance for Tourism", coordinated by the LP together with EPLO, WP3 leader.

In December, an awareness activity aimed at APs, future APs that have shown interest in participating in the project and stakeholders involved was put on the calendar, which took the form of the D.1.4.1. “Enhancing governance together for a smarter and greener tourism in the Mediterranean. Digital Awareness Day" (30/11/23) that counted with a very satisfactory participation and the presence of the JS. This work session also was a preparatory activity for the Med Talks (05/12/23). During this period, two key events were presented under the Network of Sustainable Tourism Observatories organised by REGLAZ and ELA FPA in close collaboration with the European University of Rome (WP2):
1.	Online Webinar (28/03):
Focused on strengthening governance in sustainable tourism through data, the webinar brought together 80 participants, including representatives from D4T and C4T projects, former members of the NSTO (e.g., University of Maribor, Slovenia), partners of the TPs of the ST Mission (NaTour4CChange, Herit Adapt and AQUAMAN), APs of the D4T Project (e.g., Ministry of Regional Development and EU Funds of the Republic of Croatia), other Missions (Dialogue4LivingAreas Project), and researchers from the European University of Rome. Discussions centered on enhancing governance in ST via data-driven approaches.
2.	Hybrid Event, Brussels (14/05, Lazio Region Headquarters):
This session brought together policymakers, experts, and stakeholders from across the Med to promote data-driven governance in ST. Building on the outcomes of the first webinar, this event focused on enhancing institutional cooperation, improving data-sharing strategies, and fostering innovation.
Roundtable 1 – “Data-Driven Decision-Making for ST”: Contributions from TOURISMO, LIBECCIO, and C4T partners highlighted how observatories can strengthen tourism planning through integrated data systems and emerging technologies by presenting the main achievements of their projects.
 Roundtable 2 – “Integrating ST into Policy”: Led by the European University of Rome, experts shared best practices from PANORAMED, the AQUAMAN Project (representative of the partner from La Laguna University, Canary Islands, Spain), and the Danube Strategy Point, illustrating how local and regional case studies inform national and transnational tourism policies. This event also marked the launch of two Working Groups:
1-	Data-Driven Decision-Making for Sustainable Tourism
2-	Recommendations for Policy Integration of Sustainable Tourism Practices
During the Brussels event, the 4th Steering Committee Meeting (SCM) of the project was also organized (15/05), with ELA FPA and REGLAZ coordinating the agenda, meeting logistics, and production of minutes. All partners participated, whether in person or via online.
